question: State the four functions of the human skeleton.
accept: holds the body up
protects organs e.g. skull protects brain
helps the body move
produces blood cells in bone marrow
misconceptions: it pumps blood around the body

question: What is a skeleton?
answer: the framework of bones that supports and protects the body
accept: the frame of bones that holds up and protects the body
misconceptions: it is a single solid bone

question: Explain why bones are hard.
accept: calcium makes them hard
misconceptions: they are hard because they are dry

question: Explain how the spine carries out the support function of the skeleton.
accept: it holds the body up so you can stand straight

question: Describe how the skull and ribs carry out the protection function of the skeleton.
accept: skull guards the brain; ribs guard the lungs

question: Explain why bones need to make new blood cells.
answer: bone marrow inside the bones makes new red and white blood cells
blood cells do not live very long
so they need to be replaced regularly
accept: red blood cells do not last long and must be replaced
misconceptions: bone marrow makes blood (not blood cells)

question: What is made in the bone marrow?
accept: new blood cells

question: What is a joint?
answer: a place where two bones meet (and the body can bend)
accept: where two bones join together
misconceptions: where a muscle joins a bone

question: Explain why we need joints between our bones to move.
answer: bones are very hard and cannot bend
so gaps (joints) between bones are needed to allow movement
accept: bones don't bend, so joints let the body bend
misconceptions: joints store synovial fluid for energy

question: What is the name of the material that covers the ends of the bones in a joint?
Explain how cartilage and synovial fluid reduce friction in a joint.
answer: cartilage covers the ends of the bones
it lets the bones slide over each other / protects them
synovial fluid is a lubricant that stops the bones rubbing together
accept: cartilage cushions the bone ends; synovial fluid oils the joint
misconceptions: synovial fluid glues the bones in place

question: What would happen to a joint if there was no cartilage?
answer: the ends of the bones would rub together
this would wear away the bones / cause pain when moving
accept: bones would grind and it would hurt to move
misconceptions: blood cells could not be made

question: What is the job of ligaments in a joint?
accept: they tie the bones together to keep the joint steady
misconceptions: they cover the ends of the bones

question: Which structure holds the bones together in a joint?
misconceptions: tendons hold bones together at a joint
distractors: a common misconception: tendons join muscle to bone, not bone to bone
cartilage covers and protects the bone ends, it does not hold them together
synovial fluid lubricates the joint, it does not connect the bones

question: Which mineral makes bones hard?
misconceptions: iron is thought to harden bones
students confuse oxygen with a mineral
distractors: iron is found in red blood cells, not what hardens bone
oxygen is a gas carried by blood, not a bone mineral
carbon is not the mineral that makes bones hard

question: What force does synovial fluid reduce in a joint?
misconceptions: weight is confused with a force in the joint
distractors: gravity pulls the whole body down, it is not what rubs bones together
weight is the pull of gravity on mass, not the rubbing force in a joint
upthrust acts in fluids like water, not between bones in a joint

question: A doctor examines a joint where the cartilage has worn away. Which problem is the patient most likely to have?
answer: Pain and difficulty moving the joint
misconceptions: thinking the bones would no longer be held together
thinking blood cell production would stop
thinking the muscles would get weaker
options: The bones would no longer be held together
Pain and difficulty moving the joint
The bone marrow would stop making blood cells
The muscles attached would get weaker
distractors: ligaments, not cartilage, hold bones together, so they would still be connected
blood cells are made in bone marrow, which is unaffected by worn cartilage
muscle strength is not caused by cartilage in the joint

question: The human body is made up of 206 bones. The skull and ribs together make up 46 of these. How many of the body's bones are NOT in the skull or ribs?
accept: 160 bones
misconceptions: adding instead of subtracting to get 252
using a wrong total of bones

question: A model skeleton has 206 bones. A teacher labels 24 of them on a diagram. What fraction of the bones are labelled? Give your answer as a percentage to the nearest whole number.
accept: 12 percent
about 12%
misconceptions: dividing the wrong way round to get a huge number

question: What is a tissue?
answer: a group of (the same) specialised cells working together
accept: lots of the same type of cell working together
misconceptions: a group of different organs working together

question: Explain why many muscle cells join together to form muscle tissue.
answer: one muscle cell is not very strong / cannot pull with much force
joining many cells together lets them pull with much more force
accept: together the cells can do more work or apply a stronger force
misconceptions: one muscle cell is already very strong
the cells join to make blood

question: What is an organ?
answer: a group of (more than one) tissues working together to perform the same function
accept: different tissues working together for one job

question: Explain why the heart is described as an organ and not a tissue.
answer: it is made of more than one tissue
e.g. muscle tissue, nerve tissue, connective tissue and capillaries working together
accept: it contains several different tissues that work together to pump blood
misconceptions: it is made of only one tissue
an organ and a tissue are the same thing

question: Name two tissues found in the heart and give the job of each.
answer: cardiac muscle tissue contracts to pump the blood
nerve tissue controls the speed of the heartbeat (accept: capillaries supply blood/nutrients; connective tissue holds tissues together)
accept: muscle tissue pumps blood; nerve tissue controls heart rate
misconceptions: the heart is made of only one tissue

question: Put these levels of organisation in order, from smallest to largest: organ, cell, organ system, tissue.
accept: cells then tissues then organs then organ systems
misconceptions: starting with tissue instead of cell

question: In the worked example, a muscle cell forms muscle tissue, which forms the biceps. What level of organisation is the biceps?
misconceptions: confusing an organ with an organ system
thinking the biceps is just a tissue
thinking the biceps is a single cell
distractors: a tissue is just the muscle cells together; the biceps also contains connective tissue, making it an organ
the muscular-skeletal system (all muscles and bones) is the organ system, not the single biceps
a cell is the smallest unit; the biceps is made of many cells

question: Using the muscular-skeletal system as an example, describe how a muscle cell builds up into an organ system.
answer: a muscle cell joins with many others to form muscle tissue
muscle tissue and connective tissue form an organ such as the biceps
all the muscles and bones (organs) together form the muscular-skeletal organ system
accept: cell to tissue to organ (biceps) to organ system (muscles and bones)
misconceptions: an organ and an organ system are the same
tissue forms directly into an organ system
one cell forms an organ on its own

question: What is the main function of every muscle cell?
accept: to contract
to shorten and pull

question: Why do muscles have to work in antagonistic pairs?
answer: because a muscle can only pull, not push
options: because a muscle can only pull, not push
because two muscles are stronger than one
because one muscle controls the bone and the other controls the joint
because muscles get tired and need to take turns
distractors: strength is not the reason; the reason is that pulling only moves a bone one way
muscles do not divide jobs between bone and joint like this
tiredness is not why they are paired; it is because they can only pull
misconceptions: muscles can push as well as pull
both muscles in a pair pull at the same time

question: Explain why a single muscle cannot move a bone back and forth on its own.
answer: a muscle can only pull (contract), it cannot push
so a second muscle is needed to pull the bone back the other way
accept: muscles only pull/squeeze, never push
another muscle pulls it back
misconceptions: the muscle pushes the bone back
the muscle relaxes and pushes the bone

question: What is the name for two muscles where one contracts as the other relaxes?
accept: antagonistic muscles

question: Describe what happens to the biceps and triceps when you bend your arm.
accept: biceps shortens and triceps lengthens
misconceptions: both the biceps and triceps contract
the triceps contracts to bend the arm

question: Describe what happens to the biceps and triceps when you straighten your arm.
accept: triceps shortens and biceps lengthens
misconceptions: the biceps contracts to straighten the arm

question: When the forearm moves up to bend the arm, which muscle is contracting?
distractors: the triceps contracts to straighten the arm, not bend it
only one muscle contracts at a time, not both
a muscle must contract to move the bone

question: What structure joins a muscle to a bone?
accept: tendons

question: Explain how a contracting muscle makes a bone move.
answer: the muscle is joined to the bone by a tendon
when the muscle contracts it pulls on the tendon, which pulls the bone and moves it
accept: the tendon pulls the bone as the muscle shortens
misconceptions: the muscle pushes the bone directly

question: Which type of muscle is attached to the skeleton and is under conscious control?
distractors: cardiac muscle makes up the heart and is not under conscious control
smooth muscle lines hollow passages and is not under conscious control
branched describes the structure of cardiac muscle, not a separate type
misconceptions: the heart is made of skeletal muscle
we consciously control all muscle types

question: Which type of muscle is the heart made of?
accept: heart muscle / cardiac muscle

question: Give two features of cardiac muscle.
answer: it never gets tired / we do not consciously control it
accept: beats all the time / not under conscious control / branched
misconceptions: it gets tired easily like skeletal muscle

question: Which type of muscle lines hollow passages such as the intestines and works slowly without tiring?
accept: smooth muscle tissue

question: State one difference between skeletal muscle and cardiac muscle in how they tire.
accept: skeletal muscle tires but cardiac muscle does not
misconceptions: cardiac muscle tires faster than skeletal muscle

question: Which two types of muscle are NOT under our conscious control?
distractors: skeletal muscle IS under conscious control, so this is wrong
skeletal muscle IS under conscious control, so this is wrong
smooth muscle is also not consciously controlled, so this is incomplete
misconceptions: we can consciously control the heart

question: Where do muscles get the energy they need to contract, and what is the common error to avoid?
answer: energy is released from respiration, not created/made by it
accept: energy is released by respiration

question: Why do muscle cells contain lots of mitochondria?
answer: so they can release lots of energy from respiration for movement/contraction
accept: to release plenty of energy by respiration

question: A pupil says 'When I lift my forearm, the muscle doing the work is in my forearm.' Correct this statement.
answer: the working muscle is in the upper arm, not the forearm
the upper arm muscle (biceps) pulls on the forearm bone to move it
accept: the biceps in the upper arm pulls the forearm up
misconceptions: the forearm muscles lift the forearm
the muscle and the bone it moves are in the same place

question: When one muscle in an antagonistic pair contracts, describe what happens to its shape and to the other muscle.
answer: the contracting muscle gets shorter and thicker
the other muscle relaxes and gets longer and thinner
accept: one shortens and thickens while the other lengthens and thins
misconceptions: the relaxed muscle gets shorter and thicker

question: What does it mean when we say a muscle is 'relaxed'?
answer: it is not pulling / it has lengthened
accept: it is not contracting
it is longer and not pulling

question: True or false: muscles can push. Explain your answer fully.
answer: muscles can only contract and pull, they cannot push
to move a bone the other way a second muscle in an antagonistic pair pulls it back
accept: false because muscles only pull; the partner muscle pulls the bone back
misconceptions: true, muscles push when they relax
a relaxing muscle pushes the bone

question: Name the muscle at the back of the upper arm that straightens the arm.
accept: triceps

question: On a graph axis there is a difference of 20 N between two lines, with 5 small squares between them. What is the value of one small square?
On a graph axis the values go up in 1s with 5 small squares between each labelled line. What is the value of one small square?
On a results graph, which axis (x or y) shows the thing you changed, such as shoulder strength?
accept: x-axis (horizontal axis)

question: What makes a good line of best fit on a graph where the points are spread out?
answer: it should go close to all the points
it should have an equal number of points above and below the line
accept: a straight line near all points with roughly equal points each side
misconceptions: it should be drawn dot to dot joining the points
